heading: Example 8.1
text:
Problem: A constant force acts on an
object of mass5 kg for a duration of
2 s. It increases the object’s velocity
from3 m/s to7 m/s. Find the
magnitude of the applied force. Now, if
the force was applied for a duration of
5 s, what would be the final velocity of
the object?
Solution:
We have been given that u = 3 m/s
and v = 7 m/s, t = 2 s and m = 5 kg.
F = 5 kg (7 m/s - 3 m/s)/2 s = 10 N.
duration of5 s (t = 5 s), then the final
v =u +
v = 13 m/s.
Answer: 13m/s m/s

heading: Example 8.2
text:
Problem: Which would require a
greater force -- accelerating a2 kg mass
at5 m s-2 or a4 kg mass at2 m s-2 ?
Solution:
From Eq. (8.4), we have F = ma.
Here we have m = 2 kg; a = 5 m s-2
and m = 4 kg; a = 2 m s-2.
Thus, F = m a = 2 kg * 5 m s-2 = 10 N;
and F = m a = 4 kg * 2 m s-2 = 8 N.

heading: Example 8.3
text:
Problem: A motorcar is moving with a
velocity of108 km/h and it takes4 s to
stop after the brakes are applied.
Calculate the force exerted by the
brakes on the motorcar if its mass along
with the passengers is1000 kg.
Solution:
u = 108 km/h
= 108 * 1000 m/(60 * 60 s)
= 30 m/s
v = 0 m/s.
with its passengers = 1000 kg and the
time taken to stop the motorcar, t = 4 s.
F = 1000 kg * (0 - 30) m/s/4 s
= - 7500 kg m s-2 or - 7500 N.
Answer: 0m/s m/s

heading: Example 8.4
text:
Problem: A force of 5 N gives a mass
m , an acceleration of10 m s-2 and a
1
mass m , an acceleration of20 m s-2.
2
What acceleration would it give if both
the masses were tied together?
94
Solution:
From Eq. (8.4) we have m = F/a ; and
m = F/a . Here, a = 10 m s-2;
a = 20 m s-2 and F = 5 N.
Thus, m = 5 N/10 m s-2 = 0.50 kg; and
m = 5 N/20 m s-2 = 0.25 kg.
m = 0.50 kg + 0.25 kg = 0.75 kg.
be, a = F/m = 5 N/0.75 kg = 6.67 m s-2.
